name: Lines and Angles — The Building Blocks
whatIsIt: Geometry starts with simple objects. A POINT is a dot. A LINE goes forever both ways; a RAY starts at a point and goes one way; a SEGMENT has two endpoints. Two lines can be PARALLEL (never meet, like railway tracks) or PERPENDICULAR (meet at a right angle, 90°). Angles come in types: acute (<90°), right (=90°), obtuse (90–180°), straight (=180°), reflex (>180°).
whyNeeded: Every shape, building, road and design is built from lines and angles. Naming them correctly is the shared language for all geometry — triangles, circles, coordinates and proofs.
realLifeUses: The corner of a book or wall is a right angle
A partly open door makes different angle sizes
Road junctions and ramps use angles

vocabulary.meaning: Lines that stay the same distance apart and never meet.
Lines that cross at a right angle (90°).
A ray starts at a point and goes one way; a segment has two endpoints.

story.title: The Ramp and the Rails
story.text: Ishan looks at railway tracks. 'They never meet!' 'Right — parallel lines,' says Engineer Didi. Then she points at the platform edge meeting a pillar. 'That corner is exactly 90°.' 'A right angle — the lines are perpendicular!' says Ishan. Didi nods: 'Two lines, two very different relationships.'
story.extractedProblem: Railway tracks never meet and stay the same distance apart. What are such lines called?

visual.caption: A square's corners are right angles (90°) — its opposite sides are parallel and adjacent sides perpendicular.

teachingGallery.title: Angle types and line pairs
teachingGallery.note: Compare each angle to a right angle (90°).

examples.caption: A rectangle: 90° corners, parallel opposite sides.
Tracks = parallel; a plus sign = perpendicular.

standardMethod.summary: Name the object (point/line/ray/segment), compare angles to 90°, and check line pairs for parallel or perpendicular.
steps: Line goes both ways forever; ray one way from a point; segment has two ends.
Angle type by comparing to a right angle: acute < 90°, right = 90°, obtuse 90–180°, straight = 180°, reflex > 180°.
Parallel: same distance apart, never meet.
Perpendicular: cross at exactly 90° (a right angle).

workedExamples.problem: An angle measures 120°. What type is it?
steps: 120° is more than 90° but less than 180°.

workedExamples.problem: Two lines cross making a 90° angle. What are they?
steps: A 90° crossing is a right angle.
Lines that meet at a right angle…

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a wide angle 'acute' or a sharp angle 'obtuse'.
commonMistakes.fix: Acute is SHARP and small (< 90°); obtuse is WIDE (90–180°). Compare to a right-angle corner.
Parallel = never meet (tracks). Perpendicular = meet at 90° (a plus sign or corner).

easy.q: An angle of exactly 90° is called…
Railway tracks that never meet are…

challenge.q: Ravi calls a 150° angle acute. What's the fix?

questions.q: The corner of a book is a…
Lines that stay the same distance and never meet are…

teachBackPrompt: Walk around a room with a grown-up and point out a right angle (a corner), an acute and an obtuse angle (a partly open door or scissors), a pair of parallel lines and a pair of perpendicular lines — naming each one.

revisionCard.summary: Point, line (both ways), ray (one way), segment (two ends). Angles vs 90°: acute < 90, right = 90, obtuse 90–180, straight = 180, reflex > 180. Parallel = never meet (tracks); perpendicular = cross at 90° (corner/plus).

rememberIt.hook: Angles on a line = 180. Around a point = 360. Vertically opposite are equal
rememberIt.unpack: Parallel lines cut by a line: corresponding (F-angles) and alternate (Z-angles) are equal.

studentQuestions.q: What do angles on a straight line add to?
studentQuestions.a: 180 degrees. So if one is 130, the other is 50.
The equal angles opposite each other where two lines cross - always equal.
